footer.tagline: Training and agents for teams that want to stay in control. Small, sober, honest.
footer.lockinLine: No hype. No lock-in by default.

home.metaTitle: GroeimetAI — Build agents you actually understand
home.metaDescription: GroeimetAI trains teams to build, run and maintain AI agents. No black box, no lock-in, no hype.

hero.lead: We teach teams to build agents they actually understand, run and adapt themselves. No black box. No lock-in. No hype.

problem.title1: Most teams don't have an AI problem.
problem.lead: Pilots without follow-up. Tools without an owner. Demos that feel impressive but never land in actual work. Sound familiar?
problem.card1Title: Too many tools, too little direction
problem.card1Body: Everyone is trying something different. Nothing converges into a shared way of working.
problem.card2Body: Slick demos feel powerful, but no one knows who maintains them or when they're allowed to be wrong.
problem.card3Body: Teams talk about agents, not about processes, risks and where the first real win actually is.

pillars.title: Train. Build. Embed.
pillars.lead: We're not chasing 'transformation'. We're after teams that can build, run and tune their own agents.
pillars.p1Desc: For teams that want to understand what an agent actually is, and want to build one that lightens their work.
p1Items: Your own use case as the through-line
pillars.p2Desc: For situations where a first agent has to land in production — connected to your real systems, not a demo database.
p2Items: Build with your own team alongside
pillars.p3Desc: For organisations that want management and execution on the same page — before the first tool is purchased.

anatomy.title: Three things. No more.
anatomy.lead: If you understand this, you'll understand every agent that comes next — independent of framework, independent of hype.
anatomy.card1Body: Your documentation, tone of voice, examples, policies. Not vague training data — concrete files that you maintain.
anatomy.card2Body: One document with rules, edge cases and goals. Readable by humans, executable by the model.
anatomy.card3Body: Concrete actions: send an email, look up an invoice, book a meeting. Nothing more than what you allow.

proof.title: No testimonials.
proof.body: Architects run Serac — our open source toolkit for agents — in production without us ever speaking. That's the proof. Read the code, fork it, or build on top.
proof.cardDesc: Agent framework for ServiceNow flows. Tool calls, audit log, human approval where it matters.

casesPreview.c1Snippet: Widget that auto-classifies requests, shows live status and surfaces KB articles. Implemented at multiple clients.
casesPreview.c2Title: Social media agent with Telegram approval

casesPreview.c3Snippet: Pulls bunq + Mollie, classifies each line, offers a local dashboard. Learns from corrections each quarter.

cta.title1: AI doesn't need to be bigger.
cta.lead: One intro call. We look at your team, your systems and your real question. No sales funnel, no forecast.

agents.metaDescription: We build agents alongside your team — on your stack, with no lock-in. Yours to run and adapt afterwards.

head.lead: No black-box demos. We build alongside your team, on your stack, and explain every line. After delivery you can adapt them yourself.

model.title1: An agent is not a product.
model.lead: Once you understand what an agent is made of, you can build, debug and extend one yourself. We teach you the pattern.
model.card1Body: One folder with markdown files. Documentation, policies, examples, tone of voice. Versionable in git, editable by anyone.
model.card2Body: One readable document. Goals, rules, edge cases and escalation paths. No YAML spaghetti, no 47-step flowchart.
model.card3Body: Functions in your own code. Send mail, create a ticket, look up an invoice. With permissions, audit log and human review where it counts.

process.title: Four phases. Your team is on board the whole way.
process.lead: We don't build for you — we build with you. Otherwise you end up with a tool nobody understands six months from now.
process.step1Body: We map 1-3 use cases. Which fits an agent, which is just a script? What's the value, what are the risks?
process.step2Body: The team building or maintaining the agent gets the foundation first. Folder, instructions, tools — no magic.
process.step3Body: Pair-building. We steer, you type. Or vice versa. First working version in weeks, not quarters.
process.step4Body: Handover, runbook, monitoring. What do you do if it gets something wrong? Who approves new versions?

trifecta.lead: A term from Simon Willison. An agent that has all three of these properties at once is a prompt-injection target. We design our agents so at least one is always missing — or so there's a human in the loop at the point where it matters.
trifecta.card1Body: The agent reads your documents, customer list, CRM, email threads. Fine on its own — until you add 2 and 3.
trifecta.card2Body: Web pages, customer uploads, third-party emails, scraped data. An attacker can hide instructions in there which the agent will follow.
trifecta.card3Body: The agent can reach out: send mail, call APIs, post messages. The channel an attacker uses to exfiltrate your data.
trifecta.footer: Combine all three and you've built a data-exfiltration pipeline. We design every agent explicitly to prevent that — separated capabilities, an audit log per tool call, human approval on the dangerous steps.

boundaries.title: Honest about the boundaries.
boundaries.lead: We're not for everyone. Signals that another partner suits you better:
boundaries.item1Rest: — without clarity on what it should do or who owns it.
boundaries.item2Rest: — without first looking at which work is actually worth removing.
boundaries.item3Rest: — we only build with teams. Otherwise it becomes shadow IT.
boundaries.item4Rest: — agents in customer contact need to be predictable. Period.

cta.eyebrow: Got a use case in mind?
cta.title2: We'll tell you honestly if an agent fits.

features.title: Not the standard 'AI workshop'.
features.lead: What every training has in common — regardless of length or level.
features.f1Body: You work with real tools, on your own case.
features.f2Body: Documentation you'll still use in six months.
features.f3Title: Your own use case as through-line
features.f3Body: We build something you can put to work tomorrow.
features.f4Body: You get the person who also builds it in production.

faq.title: What clients want to know upfront.

items.q: Do you also offer open trainings?
items.a: No. We only work in-company. Training really lands when participants can work on their own use cases with colleagues.
It depends on your context. We're not tied to one vendor and treat Anthropic, OpenAI, Google and open source models on equal footing.

items.q: What's the minimum number of participants?
items.a: For hands-on training: 6 people. For strategic sessions: from 3, usually board + L&D + key stakeholder.

items.q: Do we get anything to take with us?
items.a: Yes. Alongside the working agent from the hands-on day you get reference material, templates and a runbook you'll still use in six months.

items.q: Do you also train in English?
items.a: Yes. NL by default, EN on request.

cta.title1: Tell us about your team. We'll shape

cases.metaDescription: Recent agents I built — client work and personal tooling. Folder + instructions + tools, not a black box.

head.lead: For every project — client work and personal tooling — I build an agent the same way: a folder with instructions and local tools. Here are a few recent ones.

cases.listFooter: + a handful more agents not listed here — I build one for every project, so the list keeps growing.

list.title: AI classification for the Service Portal
list.summary: A Service Portal widget that automatically classifies incoming requests (incident / service request / question / HR), shows live status, surfaces relevant KB articles and generates contextual follow-up questions. Auto-detects NL or EN. Implemented at multiple clients inside their own ServiceNow environment.
list.why: Letting users pick the right form themselves didn't work — too complex. A widget handles classification well enough, and the service desk reviews where it matters. The code lives inside the client's environment; we share the pattern, not the codebase.

list.title: Social media agent with Telegram approval
list.summary: One folder that manages ideas, drafts and publications for three brands (Niels, GroeimetAI, Snow-Flow). Slash commands for /idea, /draft, /publish. Headless flow with a Telegram approval step.
list.why: No dashboard, no subscription. A folder with instructions and slash commands that can run manually or autonomously — depending on how much you want to hand off.

list.title: VAT-return agent with localhost dashboard
list.summary: Pulls transactions from bunq and Mollie, classifies them (business / mixed / private) with reason and proposal. Localhost dashboard to interactively refine. Learns from corrections each quarter.
list.why: Bookkeeping belongs local and explainable. The agent does the work, I approve at the rule level. No SaaS asking for a credit card on every new heuristic.

list.title: Weekly timesheet agent for the Dutch hours criterion
list.summary: Reads commits from the `groeimetai` GitHub org and Google Calendar, applies session inference to coding gaps, merges with manual entries, writes a markdown ledger per ISO week. Runs every Friday 17:00 via launchd.
list.why: The Dutch tax office wants provable hours. An agent that reads the existing systems weekly does that better than an Excel nobody maintains.

list.title: Daily market analysis via TradingView MCP
list.summary: Reads live charts from TradingView Desktop via a local MCP server (78 tools), scans 12 assets per day, produces bias + key levels + signal-or-wait + risk plan. Pure analysis, no orders.
list.why: Decision support belongs local and transparent. Every analysis is a markdown file you can revisit. No black box, no broker API.

about.metaDescription: GroeimetAI is deliberately small. One builder, one conversation partner. No funnel, no pipeline.

head.lead: One person in the seat that matters. Niels van der Werf — builder, trainer, conversation partner. No account manager, no handover, no PowerPoint army.

niels.para1Pre: I've been building since models were called 'GPT-3'. First for myself, then for clients. What I learned in those years: technology is almost never the problem. It's about
niels.para2: That's why GroeimetAI is deliberately small. No funnel, no pipeline. Just personal work with teams that genuinely want to change something — and understand it takes time.
niels.para3: Alongside client work I make open source. Serac is the most visible example: an agent framework for ServiceNow that I started for one client and then put on GitHub under Elastic v2. If I promise clients no lock-in, I have to live by it.

beliefs.title: Five things we don't change.
beliefs.lead: Sometimes we disagree with a prospective client. That's OK. Better honest upfront than unhappy halfway through.

items.d: Customer contact, legal, financial — no agent decides on those alone. Period.
We build on the most appropriate stack, not on the one we earn commission from (we don't get any anyway).
If you can't read it after handover, our work isn't done.
If one training turns you into the builders, that always beats handing over a finished product.
We don't grow for the sake of growth. We want to stay sharp, not scalable.

oss.lead: Not from ideology — from principle. If we promise clients no lock-in, what we build has to be removable too.

card.desc: Agent framework for ServiceNow. Orchestrates flows with tool calls, audit log and human approval where it matters.

head.lead: 30-45 minutes. You tell me where you're stuck, I tell you honestly if we can help — or if another partner is a better fit.
